content:
# James Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 264 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JAS_004_page_001.jpg

1. From where come wars and from where come fightings among you? Is it not from this — from your pleasures that wage war in your members?

2. You desire and do not have; you murder and are jealous and are not able to obtain; you fight and wage war. You do not have because you do not ask.

3. You ask and do not receive, because you ask wrongly, so that you may spend it on your pleasures.

4. Adulteresses! Do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God? Therefore whoever chooses to be a friend of the world makes himself an enemy of God.

5. Or do you suppose that the Scripture speaks to no purpose? The Spirit that God caused to dwell in us yearns jealously.

6. But he gives greater grace. Therefore it says, "God opposes the proud, but gives grace to the humble."

7. Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.

8. Draw near to God, and he will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, you sinners, and purify your hearts, you double-minded.

9. Lament and mourn and weep. Let your laughter be turned to mourning and your joy to gloom.

10. Humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will exalt you.

11. Do not speak against one another, brothers. The one who speaks against a brother or judges his brother speaks against the law and judges the law. But if you judge the law, you are not a doer of the law but a judge.

12. There is one Lawgiver and Judge, the one who is able to save and to destroy. But who are you who judge your neighbor?

13. Come now, you who say, "Today or tomorrow we will go into this city and spend a year there and trade and make a profit" —

14. you who do not know what tomorrow will bring. For you are a mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes.

15. Instead you ought to say, "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that."

16. But now you boast in your arrogance. All such boasting is evil.

17. Therefore, to the one who knows to do good and does not do it, to him it is sin.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Verse 1:** All three translations agree on the core meaning. EMET preserves the wooden "the pleasures of you, the ones warring in the members of you." PNEUMA paraphrases as "the whole army of selfish desires that wage war within you," adding interpretive color ("army," "selfish"). LOGOS strikes the best balance: "your pleasures that wage war in your members." The consensus follows LOGOS, which captures the Greek construction faithfully while reading naturally.

**Verse 2:** All three preserve the Sinaiticus reading phoneute ("you murder") rather than the variant phthoneite ("you envy"). EMET renders literally "because of the not asking of you." LOGOS and PNEUMA smooth this to "because you do not ask." PNEUMA significantly expands the verse with interpretive additions ("so you scheme," "to take it from them"). The consensus follows LOGOS, which is faithful and clear without addition.

**Verse 3:** Strong agreement across all three. EMET's "badly you ask" is too wooden; LOGOS's "you ask wrongly" and PNEUMA's "your motives are all wrong" bracket the range. The consensus follows LOGOS ("you ask wrongly") as the closest equivalent of kakos aiteisthe.

**Verse 4:** EMET preserves "Adulteresses" (moichalides); LOGOS likewise; PNEUMA renders "You unfaithful people," losing the metaphorical force of the feminine address. The consensus retains "Adulteresses" as both EMET and LOGOS agree, and the Old Testament bridal-metaphor is significant. For the second clause, EMET has "might purpose," LOGOS "wills," PNEUMA "chooses." The consensus uses "chooses" (from PNEUMA) as it best captures the deliberate act implied by boulethen. PNEUMA adds "I say it again" (not in the Greek); this is excluded.

**Verse 5:** This is the most difficult verse in the chapter. EMET reads the verb as katokesen (aorist: "dwelt"), preserving ambiguity about whether pneuma is the human spirit or the Holy Spirit. LOGOS reads katokisen (causative: "he caused to dwell") and renders "the spirit which he caused to dwell in us yearns jealously." PNEUMA follows the same causative reading: "The Spirit that God caused to dwell in us yearns jealously over us." The consensus follows the LOGOS/PNEUMA agreement on the causative reading but omits PNEUMA's added "over us." For "emptily/to no purpose/without meaning," the consensus adopts LOGOS's "to no purpose."

**Verse 6:** Strong agreement. EMET's "greater grace he gives" is overly literal in English word order. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "he gives greater grace." PNEUMA adds "to meet that need" (interpretive gloss). The consensus follows LOGOS. All three agree on the Proverbs 3:34 quotation.

**Verse 7:** Near-identical across all three. EMET and LOGOS include "therefore"; PNEUMA uses "So then." The consensus follows the EMET/LOGOS majority with "therefore."

**Verse 8:** EMET and LOGOS agree closely ("Draw near to God"). PNEUMA renders "Come close to God, and God will come close to you," substituting "God" for the pronoun "he" and adding interpretive expansion about "loyalty to God and loyalty to the world" for "double-minded." The consensus follows the EMET/LOGOS agreement. PNEUMA's "Wash your hands" versus "Cleanse your hands" — katharisate means "cleanse/purify," so "cleanse" is preferred (EMET/LOGOS).

**Verse 9:** EMET has "Be wretched"; LOGOS has "Lament"; PNEUMA paraphrases into three separate sentences. The Greek talaiporesate means "be miserable/lament." LOGOS's "Lament" best captures the imperative force. EMET's "dejection" and LOGOS's "gloom" both render katepheian; PNEUMA uses "gloom" as well. The consensus follows LOGOS, supported by PNEUMA on "gloom."

**Verse 10:** All three agree. EMET: "Be humbled... he will exalt you." LOGOS: "Humble yourselves... he will exalt you." PNEUMA: "Humble yourselves... he will lift you up in honor." The consensus follows the EMET/LOGOS agreement on "exalt" (hupsosi) and the reflexive "Humble yourselves" (LOGOS/PNEUMA).

**Verse 11:** Strong agreement between EMET and LOGOS. PNEUMA significantly paraphrases: "criticize and judge other believers... criticizing and judging God's law... your job is to obey the law." The consensus follows the close EMET/LOGOS agreement. EMET retains "brothers"; LOGOS also; PNEUMA has "Brothers and sisters." The Greek adelphoi is masculine but used inclusively; the consensus preserves "brothers" as the formal equivalent per EMET/LOGOS agreement.

**Verse 12:** All three agree on the core statement. EMET capitalizes "Lawgiver and Judge"; LOGOS lowercases; PNEUMA paraphrases as "God alone, who gave the law, is the Judge." The consensus capitalizes "Lawgiver" (as a divine title) following EMET, while using LOGOS's sentence structure.

**Verse 13:** Strong agreement. Minor variation: EMET/LOGOS say "this city"; PNEUMA says "this or that city." The Greek ten tende polin is "this city." EMET/LOGOS are correct. PNEUMA's "make money" for kerdeso is looser than EMET/LOGOS's "make a profit."

**Verse 14:** EMET has "vapor"; LOGOS has "mist"; PNEUMA has "morning mist." The Greek atmis can mean vapor, mist, or steam. LOGOS's "mist" is adopted. PNEUMA adds "morning" (not in the Greek). EMET preserves the Sinaiticus reading "you are a vapor" (este, second person); LOGOS and PNEUMA also follow this reading. For the opening clause, EMET has "who do not know the things of the tomorrow"; LOGOS has "you who do not know what your life will be on the morrow"; PNEUMA has "How do you know what your life will be like tomorrow?" The consensus follows LOGOS's structure but simplifies: "you who do not know what tomorrow will bring."

**Verse 15:** EMET's rendering is overly wooden. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree: "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that." LOGOS adds "you ought to say"; PNEUMA also. EMET's conditional "might will" reflects the subjunctive but is awkward in English. The consensus follows the LOGOS/PNEUMA agreement.

**Verse 16:** All three agree closely. The consensus follows LOGOS, which matches EMET in substance.

**Verse 17:** All three agree on meaning. EMET's word order ("sin it is to him") is too literal. LOGOS: "to him it is sin." PNEUMA: "for you it is sin" (shifting from third to second person). The consensus follows LOGOS's rendering, which preserves the Greek dative construction.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Category | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Consensus | Rationale |
|-------|----------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|------------------|-----------|
| 4:1 | Phrasing | "the pleasures of you, the ones warring in the members of you" | "your pleasures that wage war in your members" | "the whole army of selfish desires that wage war within you" | "your pleasures that wage war in your members" | LOGOS balances fidelity and fluency; PNEUMA adds "army" and "selfish" not in Greek |
| 4:2 | Phrasing | "because of the not asking of you" | "because you do not ask" | "because you don't ask God for it" | "because you do not ask" | EMET too wooden; PNEUMA adds "God" not explicit in Greek; LOGOS faithful and natural |
| 4:2 | Addition | (none) | (none) | "so you scheme and even kill to get it... to take it from them" | No additions | PNEUMA adds interpretive expansions not found in the Greek text |
| 4:3 | Lexical | "badly you ask" | "you ask wrongly" | "your motives are all wrong" | "you ask wrongly" | kakos = wrongly/badly; LOGOS most accurate; PNEUMA over-interprets |
| 4:4 | Lexical | "Adulteresses" | "Adulteresses" | "You unfaithful people" | "Adulteresses" | moichalides is feminine; EMET/LOGOS preserve the OT bridal metaphor; PNEUMA neutralizes it |
| 4:4 | Lexical | "might purpose" | "wills" | "chooses" | "chooses" | boulethen implies deliberate choice; "chooses" best captures volitional force |
| 4:4 | Phrasing | "an enemy of God is established" | "makes himself an enemy of God" | "becomes God's enemy" | "makes himself an enemy of God" | kathistatai (middle/passive): LOGOS captures the reflexive sense well |
| 4:4 | Addition | (none) | (none) | "I say it again" | Omitted | Not in the Greek; PNEUMA's rhetorical addition |
| 4:5 | Lexical | "emptily" | "to no purpose" | "without meaning" | "to no purpose" | kenos = empty/vain; "to no purpose" best conveys the adverbial sense in English |
| 4:5 | Textual/Interpretive | "the Spirit which dwelt in us" (katokesen — intransitive) | "the spirit which he caused to dwell in us" (katokisen — causative) | "the Spirit that God caused to dwell in us" (causative) | "the Spirit that God caused to dwell in us" | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on causative reading; manuscript is ambiguous but causative has stronger support |
| 4:5 | Phrasing | "long with envy" | "yearns jealously" | "yearns jealously over us" | "yearns jealously" | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on "yearns jealously"; PNEUMA adds "over us" (not explicit); EMET's "long with envy" is unclear |
| 4:6 | Addition | (none) | (none) | "to meet that need" | Omitted | PNEUMA's interpretive gloss; not in Greek |
| 4:7 | Phrasing | "Submit yourselves therefore" | "Submit yourselves therefore" | "So then, submit yourselves" | "Submit yourselves therefore" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA's "So then" is equivalent but minority rendering |
| 4:8 | Lexical | "Draw near" | "Draw near" | "Come close" | "Draw near" | eggisate = draw near; EMET/LOGOS agree on the more formal equivalent |
| 4:8 | Lexical | "Cleanse your hands" | "Cleanse your hands" | "Wash your hands" | "Cleanse your hands" | katharisate = cleanse/purify; "cleanse" is more precise than "wash" |
| 4:8 | Phrasing | "double-minded ones" | "you double-minded" | "you who are torn between loyalty to God and loyalty to the world" | "you double-minded" | PNEUMA's expansion is interpretive; dipsuchoi = double-minded/double-souled |
| 4:9 | Lexical | "Be wretched" | "Lament" | "Let there be tears for what you have done" | "Lament" | talaiporesate: "lament" captures the imperative mourning sense; PNEUMA paraphrases extensively |
| 4:9 | Lexical | "dejection" | "gloom" | "gloom" | "gloom" | katepheian: LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on "gloom"; EMET's "dejection" is acceptable but minority |
| 4:10 | Lexical | "he will exalt you" | "he will exalt you" | "he will lift you up in honor" | "he will exalt you" | hupsosi = exalt/lift up; EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "in honor" not in Greek |
| 4:11 | Phrasing | "brothers" | "brothers" | "Brothers and sisters" | "brothers" | adelphoi: masculine form used inclusively; EMET/LOGOS preserve formal equivalent |
| 4:11 | Paraphrase | "speaks against the law and judges the law" | "speaks against the law and judges the law" | "criticizing and judging God's law... your job is to obey the law" | "speaks against the law and judges the law" | PNEUMA significantly paraphrases; EMET/LOGOS agree on faithful rendering |
| 4:12 | Capitalization | "Lawgiver and Judge" (capitalized) | "lawgiver and judge" (lowercase) | "the Judge" (paraphrased) | "Lawgiver and Judge" (capitalized) | As divine titles for God, capitalization is warranted; EMET's convention adopted |
| 4:13 | Lexical | "make profit" | "make a profit" | "make money" | "make a profit" | kerdeso = gain/profit; EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA's "make money" is looser |
| 4:14 | Lexical | "vapor" | "mist" | "morning mist" | "mist" | atmis = vapor/mist; "mist" (LOGOS) is natural English; PNEUMA adds "morning" not in Greek |
| 4:14 | Phrasing | "for a little time appearing, and then disappearing" | "appears for a little time and then vanishes" | "appears for a little while, then vanishes" | "appears for a little while and then vanishes" | All three agree in substance; blend of LOGOS/PNEUMA phrasing |
| 4:15 | Phrasing | "If the Lord might will, and we will live, and we will do this or that" | "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that" | "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that" | "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that" | LOGOS/PNEUMA identical; EMET's "might will" is awkward in English |
| 4:17 | Phrasing | "sin it is to him" | "to him it is sin" | "for you it is sin" | "to him it is sin" | EMET too wooden; PNEUMA shifts person (3rd to 2nd); LOGOS preserves Greek dative construction |
